timing_method: MTLCommandBuffer.gpuEndTime - gpuStartTime (per-launch GPU timestamps, waitUntilCompleted each iter)

hypothesis_tested.name: Apple M3 FP16 2x boost (via MPS dedicated FP16 path)
hypothesis_tested.prediction: If Apple M3 has a dedicated FP16 MMA datapath that MPS uses internally (even if simdgroup_matrix<half> doesn't expose it), MPS FP16 throughput should reach ~2x MPS FP32 throughput (~3.4 TFLOPS at d=1024).
hypothesis_tested.alternative: If Apple M3 truly has no FP16 acceleration anywhere, MPS FP16 ~= MPS FP32 (~1x).
hypothesis_tested.verdict: FAIL — Apple M3 FP16 2x hypothesis FALSIFIED. MPS FP16 peak (1.875 TFLOPS) is only 1.10x MPS FP32 peak (1.703 TFLOPS) at d=1024. Consistent with Apple M3 having NO dedicated FP16 datapath — FP16 only gets minor benefit from halved bandwidth, not from doubled compute throughput. This independently corroborates N25's hand-emit simdgroup_matrix<half> finding (0.87x FP32, peak 789 GFLOPS): the bottleneck is not the simdgroup_matrix API, it is the silicon itself.

shapes.max_rel_err_caveat: near-zero ref values blow up the bare relative error; max_abs_diff=0.117 across N=256 elements with K=256 accumulator is consistent with normal FP16 K-loop drift (~K * ULP); numeric correctness PASS qualitatively

notes: hexa simdgroup FP16/FP32 columns from rfc075_metal_simdgroup_matmul_fp16_2026_05_21 — peak across {8x8, 16x16, 32x32_tg} kernel variants.

MPS FP16 wins clearly only at d>=768 where the small-shape overhead amortizes. At d<=512 MPS FP16 is roughly half of MPS FP32 (likely because FP16 path adds dispatch overhead but offers no compute advantage to recoup it).

MPS FP16 / hexa simdgroup FP16 = 2.4-5.4x: even with no FP16 compute boost, MPS extracts substantially more from the silicon via better tiling/scheduling than the hand-emit kernel — same asymmetry seen on the FP32 side (MPS FP32 = 1.7 TFLOPS vs hexa FP32 simdgroup peak 0.91 TFLOPS, 1.87x).
MPS FP16 peak 1.875 TFLOPS is 53.6% of the 1x-FP32 peak hypothesis and 26.8% of the 2x-FP32 peak hypothesis.

This is the same ~50% utilization MPS FP32 reaches against its own theoretical (1.70 TFLOPS / 3.5 TFLOPS = 48.6%). Consistent with MPS hitting compute-bound asymptote at d=1024 — no hidden compute headroom unlocked by FP16.
If Apple M3 had a dedicated FP16 MMA path at 2x FP32, MPS would have shown a clear 1.5x-2x throughput jump even at 50% utilization (~3.4 TFLOPS). It did not.

interpretation: Headline: MPS FP16 GEMM peaks at 1.875 TFLOPS @ d=1024, MPS FP32 peaks at 1.703 TFLOPS @ d=1024 — ratio 1.10x. The 'Apple M3 has a hidden 2x FP16 MMA path that MPS uses' hypothesis is REFUTED by this baseline.
Below d=768 MPS FP16 is significantly SLOWER than MPS FP32 (0.48-0.53x). The fp16-half-bandwidth advantage doesn't pay back the fixed dispatch/launch overhead until problem size is large enough.

Independent corroboration of N25 (hexa-emit simdgroup_matrix<half>): N25 found FP16 simdgroup peaked at 0.87x FP32 simdgroup. This MPS measurement finds the same direction (1.10x is well below 2x). Together: Apple M3 ALU silicon treats FP16 essentially the same as FP32 for matmul throughput. No hidden FP16 compute path exists.

MPS FP16 still beats hexa-emit simdgroup FP16 by 2.4-5.4x — MPS engineering (tile schedules, micro-kernels, memory layout) is far ahead of the hand-emit baseline. This is unchanged from the FP32 conclusion: closing the MPS gap is about *tiling* not *FP16 magic*.
Variance is wide at d<=768 (std 0.063-0.683 ms) — shared developer laptop. Median is the reliable estimator; 5 warmup + 50 timed protocol matches N7.
honest_scope: FP16 numeric correctness: max_abs_diff=0.117 at d=256 (K=256 accumulator) is consistent with normal FP16 matmul drift (~K * 2^-10 ULP). The max_rel_err diagnostic blows up because tiny ref values appear near zero — this is a known artifact of bare relative-error metrics on FP16 matmul, not a kernel correctness signal. We trust MPS to produce numerically valid FP16 output.
Only one shape (d=256) runs the CPU FP16 reference because cpu_ref_fp16_accum is O(N^3) and would dominate wall time at d=1024.

MPS internally may upcast FP16 -> FP32 -> FP16 (silent), in which case our measurement is 'MPS-as-actually-used-by-developers FP16'. Even so, the verdict stands: from the developer's API surface, FP16 dtype does NOT unlock 2x throughput on Apple M3.
Single host, single GPU. Variance noted. No rerun on quiesced machine — defer.
MPSDataType.float16 was accepted by MPSMatrixDescriptor / MPSMatrixMultiplication without API change — no Apple deprecation gap encountered. Build clean, run clean.
followup: Mixed-precision MPS: FP16 input + FP32 accumulator. MPS may expose this via MPSMatrixMultiplication(... resultDataType: .float32) or via the MPSNDArray API. Worth a separate fire to confirm whether mixed-precision unlocks anything FP16-pure does not.
d=2048+ shapes — does MPS FP16 maintain its small 10% advantage or scale further?
Quiesced-laptop rerun with timed=200 to tighten variance, especially d=384/512.

Cross-reference: Apple's own Metal Performance Shaders documentation for FP16 — does the documentation claim a dedicated FP16 path? If yes, this measurement contradicts Apple's claim and merits a deeper SoC characterization. If no, this confirms the silicon's homogeneous FP32-ish ALU treatment.
